Name: adaptive_kernel_fusion
Title: Adaptive Kernel Fusion for Memory Bandwidth Optimization in Neural Network Inference
Short Hypothesis: Can we dynamically adjust the fusion of CUDA kernels based on real-time memory bandwidth measurements to optimize inference latency in deep learning models?
Related Work: Current research predominantly focuses on static kernel fusion strategies. Recent work on CUDA streams and memory utilization has shown potential for optimizing kernel execution, but dynamic adaptation based on live metrics is underexplored. This proposal distinguishes itself by introducing a method that continuously monitors memory bandwidth and adjusts kernel fusion on-the-fly, as opposed to relying solely on pre-determined configurations.
Abstract: In the pursuit of efficient deep learning inference on GPUs, optimizing kernel execution through fusion has long been a key strategy. However, existing methods typically employ static fusion techniques that do not account for real-time hardware conditions, particularly memory bandwidth fluctuations. This proposal introduces Adaptive Kernel Fusion (AKF), a novel method that dynamically fuses CUDA kernels based on ongoing measurements of memory bandwidth utilization. By leveraging a lightweight profiling mechanism, AKF will adapt the fusion strategy during runtime, thus reducing stalls caused by memory bandwidth limitations. We will conduct experiments across various neural network architectures to evaluate the performance gains in terms of inference latency, throughput, and energy efficiency. Our approach promises to deliver significant improvements in real-world applications, enabling more responsive AI systems. The proposed system will be benchmarked against static kernel fusion approaches, with detailed profiling to highlight the specific hardware-level gains achieved through adaptive fusion.
Experiments: 1. Implement AKF in a controlled environment with various neural networks (e.g., ResNet, Transformer). 2. Measure and compare the latency, tokens-per-second, and energy consumption against static kernel fusion methods. 3. Use nvprof/Nsight to track occupancy, memory bandwidth, and stall reasons in both static and adaptive settings. 4. Evaluate performance under varying input sizes and batch configurations to ensure comprehensive benchmarking. 5. Validate findings with multiple GPU configurations to assess scalability and reproducibility.
Risk Factors and Limitations: 1. The overhead of real-time monitoring may offset the gains from dynamic fusion under certain workloads. 2. The complexity of implementation may increase the chance of bugs or inefficiencies in the profiling mechanism. 3. Variability in hardware performance could affect the generalizability of results across different GPU architectures.
